article_title: Last-Ever Pegasus XL Set to Launch June 27 to Save NASA's Falling Swift Telescope
summary: Clean follow-up on the June 27 last-ever Pegasus XL / Swift reboost launch; every quote, number, name, and date verified against the cited source snapshots. Only flag is a non-blocking allowlist warning for two reputable sources.

content_preview.title: Last-Ever Pegasus XL Set to Launch June 27 to Save NASA's Falling Swift Telescope
content_preview.summary: Rocket and aircraft integration is complete for the June 27 air-launch of Katalyst's LINK servicer, riding the final flight of Northrop Grumman's Pegasus XL to reboost the sinking Swift Observatory.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The robotic spacecraft NASA is counting on to rescue its falling Swift Observatory is now mated to its rocket and ready to fly. Katalyst Space Technologies' LINK servicer will launch on a Northrop Grumman Pegasus XL air-launched rocket later this month from Kwajalein Atoll in the Marshall Islands, [NASA wrote](https://science.nasa.gov/blogs/swift/2026/06/15/rocket-attached-to-aircraft-for-katalyst-nasa-swift-boost/) on its Swift mission blog. Liftoff is targeted for June 27, 2026, [...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Tight, well-structured News piece (787 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / The Last Pegasus / What We Don't Know structure. The 'What We Don't Know' section honestly bounds the claims (capture is post-launch, slot is weather/range-dependent, extension length is unspecified) without speculation.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 6 source snapshots hash-verified intact against manifest.json (uncompressed sha256 matched for source-0 through source-5) and personally read. source-0 (NASA Swift blog): confirms Kwajalein Atoll/Marshall Islands, June 10 integration, June 12 attached to aircraft, June 19 Wallops departure, Stargazer modified L-1011, solar-activity-driven drag lowering Swift's orbit. source-1 (NASA news release): confirms audio-only teleconference at 11 a.m. EDT Wednesday June 17 with Brad Cenko (Swift PI), Kieran Wilson (LINK PI, Katalyst), and Wes Collier (VP launch systems, Northrop Grumman); confirms rendezvous/raise-altitude/extend-science-mission. source-2 (Katalyst release): confirms the 'eight months' design/build/test claim and all three quotes verbatim with correct speakers — Ghonhee Lee (CEO), Brad Cenko (Goddard astrophysicist, Swift PI), Nicky Fox (Associate Administrator, Science Mission Directorate). source-3 (EarthSky): confirms the June 27, 2026 launch date, the 185 mi / 300 km minimum operating altitude, and 'plunge back to Earth by this fall.' source-4 (New Atlas): confirms 880 lb / 400 kg LINK, three robotic arms, optical cameras / LiDAR ranging sensors / guidance flight software / real-time imaging, ground-handling fixtures, 373 mi / 600 km target orbit, 'a comfortable number of years,' and the Steve Hollo (chief engineer of Pegasus) quote verbatim. source-5 (Wikipedia): confirms first flight April 5, 1990, '45 missions between 1990 and 2021,' B-52 initial carrier transitioning to the L-1011 Stargazer (converted airliner formerly owned by Air Canada) by 1994.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every quote, number, name, title, and date traces to a cited snapshot. Date discipline is correct: the June 27, 2026 launch date is sourced to EarthSky and NASA-aligned reporting, NOT to Wikipedia. Wikipedia's own Pegasus entry actually states the final flight is 'On the 28th of June 2026' (June 28), and the article correctly declines to use Wikipedia for the launch date, relying on it only for vehicle history (45 missions, 1990-2021, April 5 1990 debut, carrier-aircraft lineage). No hallucinations detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. 'The rescue doubles as a farewell' is the only mildly colorful phrasing and is supported by the factual end-of-program framing; no sensationalism or editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: Genuine developing-story follow-up, not a duplicate. The May 10, 2026 Machine Herald article covered the May 4 environmental-testing milestone; this piece centers on a distinct, later event — completed rocket/aircraft integration, staging at Kwajalein, the June 17 media teleconference, and the imminent June 27 launch as the last-ever Pegasus flight. The in-body cross-reference link to /article/2026-05/10-katalyst-wraps-environmental-testing-... resolves to the actual published article slug.
recommendations: Two cited domains are not on config/source_allowlist.txt: katalystspace.com (the mission's primary-source company release) and earthsky.org (an established astronomy outlet and the cited authority for the June 27 date). Both are reputable and appropriate here; the warning is non-blocking. The maintainer may wish to add these domains to the allowlist in a separate change. Per policy, the allowlist file was NOT modified during this review.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Integrity checks pass (valid Ed25519 signature, matching payload hash). All sources read and verified; every claim is supported. Correct sourcing discipline on the contested June 27 vs June 28 date. No corrections record needed — the sole finding is a non-blocking allowlist warning for two legitimate sources. Ready for publication.
